§ 05 / Build Evidence — LifeLine
Group 39 · Phoenix Cohort TS Academy PM May 2026
05Build Evidence · Engineering Documentation

Built. Tested. Shipped.

A single-file web app, deployed and tested on Nigerian mobile networks. 12 screens · 30/30 tests passing · 2 iterations.

This pack documents the technical execution behind LifeLine — the architecture, the network-resilience strategy, the screenshots from the live build, the test plan, the iteration log, and the validation framework we'd use to measure success post-launch.

Screens

Tests Pass

StackHTML · CSS · JS
MapLeaflet 1.9.4 (inlined)
DeployGitHub Pages
BundleSingle file · ~210 KB
NetworkMTN 3G / 4G
Live Demo URL tsa-phoenix-safetytech-39.github.io/lifeline/lifelineapp
What's inside MVP scope, architecture diagram, network resilience strategy, 18 real screenshots across 3 flows, full 30-case test matrix, 2-iteration log, vibe-coding evidence, validation framework, and metrics.
Demo cheatsheet Correct OTP: 8484. Bottom nav: 🔑 Onboard · 🏠 Safe Arrival · 🚨 SOS · ⚠ Failures lets the team jump to any flow in one tap during the pitch.
Vibe-coding disclosure App was vibe-coded with Claude across two iterations. All generated code reviewed by Group 39. Edge cases (CDN failure, GPS denial, OTP errors) caught via jsdom + manual device testing.
§ 05.1 / MVP Scope & Architecture
What we built and how Page 02 / 09
01 MVP Scope & System Design

One file. Zero CDNs. Built for MTN 3G.

LifeLine is a single self-contained HTML file. No build step, no framework, no CDN. Every dependency is inlined — because Nigerian mobile networks can't be trusted with critical safety infrastructure.

MVP Capabilities

What shipped.

Seven capabilities, prioritised by RICE. Everything else is V2.

#
Capability
Why it's in MVP
01
Phone-OTP authentication
Identity without passwords. Universal on Nigerian mobile. Demo code 8484.
02
Trusted contacts (2–3) with consent
The network is the product. No contacts → no app value. Hard gate before home screen.
03
Safe-arrival trip · live map · ETA countdown
Daily-use anchor. Trains the habit that makes SOS work when it matters.
04
3-second hold SOS · auto-escalation
Single deliberate emergency action. 2:45 grace window prevents accidental escalation.
05
Real OpenStreetMap · self-hosted library
Map shows real Lagos/Abuja streets even on poorest networks. Tiles degrade gracefully.
06
Three failure paths designed-in
OTP wrong, GPS denied, trip overdue — each has a recovery flow, not a dead-end error.
07
Two delighters · confetti + shake-to-SOS
Emotional payoff on safe arrival + accessibility (shake hint for low-vision users).
Technology Stack

Stack & file structure.

Deliberately boring. The interesting engineering is in the constraints, not the framework choices.

Layer
Choice
Frontend
Vanilla HTML + CSS + JS · no framework
Map library
Leaflet 1.9.4 · inlined (no CDN)
Map tiles
tile.openstreetmap.org · graceful fail
Geolocation
navigator.geolocation
Haptics
navigator.vibrate([200,100,200])
Shake detect
DeviceMotionEvent
Deployment
GitHub Pages · static
Bundle size
~210 KB · single file
lifeline/ ├── index.html — full app, single file ├── lifeline-app.html — mirror of index ├── lifeline-wireframes.html ├── LifeLine_Build_Doc.md ├── README.md └── .github/ └── workflows/ └── pages.yml — auto-deploy on push # Run locally — no build step open index.html # Deploy — already wired git push origin main
System Architecture

Architecture diagram.

The entire app lives inside one browser tab. Nothing crosses the network at runtime except OSM map tiles — and those fail safely.

UI Layer
12 screens
Dark navy palette · vanilla DOM · CSS Grid
State (in-memory)
history · selETA · tripSecs · otpAttempts
No persistence · refresh resets all
Browser APIs
navigator.geolocation
navigator.vibrate · DeviceMotionEvent
↑ ↓ ↑ ↓ ↑ ↓
Leaflet (embedded)
~147 KB inlined CSS + JS + base64 PNGs
Zero CDN dependency · loads instantly
Failure handlers
3 paths · OTP wrong · GPS denied · Trip overdue
OSM Tile Service · external
tile.openstreetmap.org — loads tiles progressively. Fails to transparent grey (no broken image icons). Critical path is NOT blocked on tiles.
GitHub Pages · static host
Static CDN — auto-deployed via Actions. Single index.html. No backend, no API, no database in MVP.
Network Resilience

Why zero CDNs matters in Nigeria.

SafeSignal (previous project) lost weeks to CDN flakiness on MTN. We refused to repeat that mistake.

Concern
Solution
CDN reliability on MTN/Airtel
Leaflet JS + CSS embedded inline. Zero external library calls at runtime.
Tile fetch failures
errorTileUrl set to transparent 1×1 GIF. Failed tiles show clean grey, never broken-image icon.
Slow tile loading
Tiles load progressively from tile.openstreetmap.org. Partial map (2–3 tiles) is enough to be useful.
Leaflet marker images
All Leaflet image assets base64-inlined in CSS. No broken-icon risk even on full disconnect.
GPS unavailable / denied
Falls back to Lagos default coords silently. Manual entry path via bottom-sheet modal.
Data Flows

Two core flows.

Both flows are entirely client-side. State lives in-memory; flows exit on user action or timeout.

FLOW 02Safe Arrival
Tap "Start safe arrival"
→
Pick destination + ETA (15/30/60)
→
Tap "Share with contacts"
→
Location permission
→
Live tracking screen
→
Countdown ticks
happy →
User taps "I've arrived safely"
→
Confetti · contacts notified · trip closes
overdue →
ETA hits 0:00
→
Red OVERDUE banner
→
Contacts auto-alerted at +5 min
FLOW 03SOS / Emergency
User holds SOS button
→
3-second SVG ring fills
→
vibrate([200,100,200])
→
SOS Active screen
→
2:45 countdown · GPS live · contacts alerted
cancel →
User taps "I'm safe — cancel"
→
Emergency Resolved screen
escalate →
Timer hits 0:00
→
Auto-nav to Contact View
→
Both contacts notified · second push
§ 05.2 / Live Build — Onboarding
Real screenshots · MTN LTE · Android Chrome Page 03 / 09
02 Live Build · Onboarding

The app, on a real phone, in Abuja.

All screenshots captured from the deployed build at tsa-phoenix-safetytech-39.github.io/lifeline on Android Chrome, MTN LTE. Status bar visible at top — these are not mockups.

01Splash

Real logo on white card over dark navy background. "Get started" CTA is teal. Bottom: demo nav bar lets pitch team jump to any flow instantly.

DEFAULT
02Onboarding · 1 of 2

First value-prop slide. "Your safety, proactively" sets the prevention-first frame. Dots indicator at top of text shows progress.

DEFAULT
03aOTP — Enter number

+234 prefix with Nigerian flag. Placeholder shows expected format. Send OTP button is full-width.

DEFAULT
03bOTP — Code boxes

After tapping Send OTP. Four boxes appear with the demo hint visible inline — judges don't need to wait for SMS.

DEFAULT
03cOTP — Code filled

8484 entered across the four boxes. Resend timer hits 0:00 and becomes tappable. Auto-advances on correct code.

FILLED
04Onboarding · 2 of 2

Second value-prop. "Share your journey" introduces the Safe Arrival mechanic before the contacts gate, so users understand why contacts are needed.

05Trusted Contacts

The hard gate. Amaka Obi (consent confirmed ✓) + Tunde James (consent pending ⏳). Pending contacts cannot receive alerts — consent is real, not a checkbox.

→Landing
Flow Exit

Finish setup → Home screen.

Onboarding complete in ~90 seconds end-to-end if user has phone in hand.

First-launch funnel is measured per Stage 8 metrics plan: % completing each step, drop-off points, time-to-first-trip.

Build evidence · onboarding

Real device. Real network. Real screenshots.

Every screenshot on this page was captured on an Android Tecno phone, running Chrome, on MTN LTE in Abuja. The status bar showing "22:26 · LTE · ●" is genuine. This is not a Figma export — this is the deployed app.

Edge cases handled
  • Wrong OTP — boxes shake red, attempts counter decrements, 3 failures → 5-minute lockout.
  • Resend timer — counts down from 0:45. Resend link only tappable at 0:00.
  • Phone format validation — short or non-numeric input shakes the field, OTP block doesn't appear.
  • Skip onboarding — both value-prop slides have a quiet "Skip" for returning users.
§ 05.3 / Live Build — Safe Arrival
The daily-habit flow Page 04 / 09
03 Live Build · Safe Arrival

Home → trip → tracking → safe.

The flow most users will run every day. Real GPS coordinates · real OSM map · real Leaflet route line. Screenshots include intermediate states (loading, GPS denied) — not just happy path.

06Home

"Good evening, Ada" + green dot status. SOS button is large but secondary. "Start safe arrival" card is the bright primary action.

DEFAULT
06bSOS ring filling

Hold-to-activate state. SVG ring fills clockwise over 3 seconds. Releasing early resets the ring — prevents pocket bumps.

ACTIVE-HOLD
07aShare Trip — modal

GPS denied or unavailable. Bottom-sheet modal slides up with manual address entry. Flow continues — no dead-end.

GPS DENIED
07bShare Trip — loading

"Requesting permission…" while browser prompt is open. Allow button shown inline for retry if user dismissed it.

LOADING
07cShare Trip — ready

GPS active. Real coords 8.99702, 7.57772 (Abuja). ETA chips · sharing-with contacts · primary "Share with contacts" CTA.

GPS ACTIVE
07dShare Trip · shake detected

Delighter — shake-to-SOS hint. Shaking the phone fires DeviceMotionEvent and shows the orange hint toast. Hold SOS still required to activate.

DELIGHTER
08Live Tracking

Real Leaflet + OSM map. Teal origin dot, red destination dot, dashed route. 29:58 countdown. "LIVE" badge pulses. 2 contacts watching.

LIVE
09Safe Confirmed

"You're safe! 🎉" Green check + confetti delighter. Contact response cards show Amaka and Tunde's actual reactions. Loop closes.

CONFETTI
Why the loading/error states matter

Failure paths build trust.

Three screenshots on this page show the app in non-happy states: GPS denied, GPS loading, shake detected. Most safety apps demo only the happy path. We demo the failure paths because real users on real networks hit them — and we wanted reviewers to see we'd thought about it.

Map technical notes
  • Leaflet 1.9.4 embedded inline as JS + CSS + base64 marker PNGs. Zero CDN dependency.
  • Tile fetch from tile.openstreetmap.org with errorTileUrl set to a 1×1 transparent GIF.
  • Tested on MTN 3G throttled — library loads instantly, tiles fill in progressively, no broken icons.
  • Route line is a Leaflet polyline with dashed pattern, animated marker glides from origin to destination as time progresses.
§ 05.4 / Live Build — Emergency (SOS)
The safety-net flow Page 05 / 09
04 Live Build · Emergency

SOS active. Contact view. Resolved.

The flow most users will never run — but every screen of it has to work perfectly. Three screens, full red-state design, contact-side view included.

10SOS Active

Full dark-red background. SOS ACTIVATED banner pulses. 2:44 countdown is the largest element. GPS status + contacts alerted shown below.

SOS ACTIVE
11Contact View

Amaka's perspective. Red-tinted OSM map · pulsing location marker · "SOS LIVE" badge. Three large action buttons: Call Ada · Message · Escalate.

CONTACT VIEW
12Emergency Resolved

Lock emoji · "Emergency resolved" · contacts' cancel-acknowledgements shown as response cards. Same pattern as Safe Confirmed — visual consistency reinforces resolution.

RESOLVED
Inside the build · SOS implementation

Code excerpt: 3-second hold + escalation.

The actual JavaScript powering the SOS button. Mouse/touch events drive an SVG ring that fills over 3000ms; release before then resets cleanly.

// SOS hold-to-activate · 3 seconds · resets on release const HOLD_MS = 3000; let holdStart = null; let rafId = null; sosBtn.addEventListener('pointerdown', () => { holdStart = performance.now(); rafId = requestAnimationFrame(tick); }); function tick() { const elapsed = performance.now() - holdStart; const pct = Math.min(elapsed / HOLD_MS, 1); ring.style.strokeDashoffset = circumference * (1 - pct); if (pct >= 1) { navigator.vibrate?.([200, 100, 200]); // haptic confirm navigateTo('sos-active'); startEscalationCountdown(165); // 2:45 return; } rafId = requestAnimationFrame(tick); } sosBtn.addEventListener('pointerup', resetRing); sosBtn.addEventListener('pointerleave', resetRing);
Why the contact view exists

The other side of the alert.

Most safety apps demo only the alert sender's screen. The contact view is where the alert actually does its work. Showing it documents that we've thought about the full system — both sender and receiver — and that contacts have a clear, fast set of actions to take when the alert lands.

Reliability built-in
  • Haptic confirm · navigator.vibrate([200,100,200]) fires the moment the 3s hold completes — confirms activation even if user can't see screen.
  • GPS captured before alert sends · contacts get last-known coordinates even if user moves out of GPS range.
  • 2:45 grace window · long enough to cancel false alarms (pocket bump that completed somehow), short enough to escalate real emergencies.
  • Resolution screen mirrors Safe Confirmed · same response-card pattern · same emotional close.
§ 05.5 / Build & Test Evidence
Deploy · Demo · 30/30 Tests Page 06 / 09
05 Build · Run · Test

30 tests. 30 passes. Two devices. Two networks.

Manual test pass on iPhone 13 mini (iOS Safari) + Tecno Camon 19 (Android Chrome), on MTN LTE and 3G-throttled. Every flow, every failure path, every delighter — verified.

Quick Start · Local

Run it.

No build step. No npm install. Just open the file.

# Clone the repo git clone https://github.com/tsa-phoenix-safetytech-39/lifeline.git cd lifeline # Open in any modern browser open index.html # Or serve locally for permissions (GPS works on https/localhost) python3 -m http.server 8000 # → http://localhost:8000
Deploy · GitHub Pages

Ship it.

Already wired. Push to main → live in ~30 seconds.

# Make any change · commit · push git add index.html git commit -m "fix: SOS countdown timer drift" git push origin main # GitHub Actions deploys automatically: # .github/workflows/pages.yml # Live at: # tsa-phoenix-safetytech-39.github.io/lifeline
Demo Cheatsheet

For pitch day.

The bottom nav bar lets the team jump to any flow in one tap — no waiting for OTP SMS, no walking 30 minutes for the countdown.

Key
Trigger
Behaviour
🔑

8484

Demo correct code. Any other 4-digit input triggers the failure state.

On a real device — fires the shake-to-SOS hint toast (delighter #2).
🔴

Press & hold the SOS button for 3 seconds to activate emergency for real.

§ 05.6 / Iteration & Vibe-Coding Evidence
How the build evolved Page 07 / 09
06 Iteration · Vibe-Coding

Two iterations. Real review. Honest reset.

The first build worked but felt thin. Group review surfaced gaps; iteration 2 added failure paths and delighters. Vibe-coded with Claude — every prompt, every fix, documented.

ITERATION 1

From design artefact → first build

Weeks 1–2
What we started with

A Figma design with 12 screens documenting the happy path for all 3 flows. ChatGPT had generated a first scaffold but it used CDN'd Leaflet and had basic styling for screens 7–12.

Gaps: no failure paths defined · no delighters · no spec for low-network resilience · function references broken between screens.

What changed in iteration 1
  • Self-hosted Leaflet inline — eliminated CDN dependency
  • Built all 12 screens to design-system specification
  • Implemented real countdown timers (tracking + SOS escalation)
  • SVG hold-to-activate ring for SOS button
  • Bottom nav bar — flow-jumping for pitch demo
Why

SafeSignal experience taught us CDN failures on MTN are real. We were not going to lose the demo to a tile.openstreetmap.org slowdown.

ITERATION 2

Failure paths + delighters

Week 3
Trigger · group review feedback

"This feels too perfect. Real users will hit errors. Where's the proof we thought about the edge cases?"

Failure paths added
  1. 01 OTP wrong code — shake animation, red error, attempts counter ("2 attempts left"), input clearing. Resend timer restarts on Resend tap.
  2. 02 Location permission denied — bottom-sheet modal lets user enter address manually. Flow continues.
  3. 03 Trip overdue — countdown becomes "OVERDUE" in red, banner notifies that contacts auto-alerted at +5 min.
Delighters added
  1. 01 Confetti on safe arrival — 30 colourful pieces with randomized colour, size, rotation, timing. Plays after "I've arrived safely" tap.
  2. 02 Shake-to-SOS hint — DeviceMotionEvent triggers a toast on real devices. Full SOS still requires the 3-second hold (prevents false alarms from pocket-bumps).
Why

Failure paths build trust — they prove we thought about real users on bad networks with denied permissions. Confetti creates a positive feedback loop that makes safe-arrival the daily anchor habit — which is what makes the emergency feature work when it matters.

Vibe-Coding Disclosure

Built with Claude. Reviewed by humans.

Full transparency on AI assistance. What was generated, what was edited, what didn't work first time, what we learned.

What worked
  • Embedding Leaflet inline (~147 KB) — solved the CDN problem definitively
  • Bottom nav bar — lets the team show any flow in 1 tap during pitch
  • Real OTP failure path with attempts counter — proof we thought about edges
  • jsdom for verification — caught silent undefined-function bugs
What didn't work first time
  • Surgical byte-offset HTML edits introduced silent function-reference bugs (called drawSosMap() after renaming to buildSosMap)
  • Substring checks for verification passed but app didn't actually work
  • Lesson · when changing structure, rewrite the file as one coherent string, don't splice
  • Lesson · validate with actual JS parsing + DOM simulation, not text matching
Key prompt patterns
PROMPT 01 ·
"Build this design, but make it work on the worst Nigerian mobile networks like you did for SafeSignal"
PROMPT 02 ·
"I tested it, it's not working. Plus where are the other artifacts?" ← led to honest reset and full ship
§ 05.7 / Validation Framework & Metrics
How we'd measure success Page 08 / 09
07 Validation & Metrics

How we'd know LifeLine is working — post-launch.

Five evidence categories. One North Star. Three supporting metrics. This is the measurement plan we'd wire up the moment we ship past MVP — everything below is instrumented in the architecture, even though MVP doesn't emit events yet.

Evidence Framework · Five Categories

What "working" actually means.

Each category answers a different question. Stack them together and you get the full picture — not just usage, but reliability + trust.

#
Category
What we'd collect
Success indicator
A
Problem Validation
User interviews · survey responses · existing pain with WhatsApp/SMS during emergencies · frequency of unsafe commutes
Users report feeling safer using LifeLine · strong interest in trusted-contact workflows · high willingness to use check-ins
B
MVP Validation
Hypothesis tests — fast emergency comms → SOS usage · trusted contacts → setup rates · check-ins → repeat usage · silent panic adoption
High retention · low deletion · repeat engagement · users complete contact setup
C
Behavioural
Weekly check-ins · live location sharing frequency · trusted contacts added · onboarding completion · referral invites sent
High repeat usage · low onboarding drop-off · referrals out · multi-contact adoption
D
Reliability
Alert delivery latency · notification success logs · escalation completion timestamps · SMS fallback success · crash recovery
Demonstrates LifeLine performs reliably during high-stress moments — alerts arrive in <5s, 99.5%+ delivery
E
Trust & Safety
Satisfaction surveys · app store ratings · privacy complaints · security incident reports · trust score / NPS
"Would you trust LifeLine in an emergency?" · "Would you recommend to family?" — both >80% yes
Behavioural Signals

What we'd watch — daily.

Two columns. Positive signals confirm we're building the right product. Negative signals are early-warning system for product-market mismatch.

✓ Positive signals
  • Repeated weekly check-ins per user (≥3 per week)
  • Frequent live location sharing — share rate >40% of trips
  • Users add multiple trusted contacts (mean ≥2)
  • Low onboarding drop-off (<15% per step)
  • Referral invitations sent to friends/family
  • Repeat SOS cancellations vs escalations — high cancel rate is good
⚠ Negative signals
  • High false-alert rate (uncancelled accidental SOS)
  • Users disabling permissions (GPS, vibrate, notifications)
  • Low escalation acknowledgment by contacts
  • Short session durations after onboarding
  • High churn within first 7 days post-onboarding
  • App uninstalls following first failed alert delivery
North Star Metric

The one number that captures whether LifeLine works.

If we get this metric right, the product is doing its job. If it stalls, something is broken — and the supporting metrics tell us where.

North Star · NSM
Successful Safety Interactions per Active User

The count of completed safety-related actions per active user, per week. Combines reliability, engagement, and trust into a single composite — measures whether LifeLine is delivering real-world product value, not just installs.

NSM  =  Total successful safety actions Active users (weekly)
Target · ≥ 3.0 per active user per week (by week 8 post-launch)
What counts as a safety action
Successful "I'm safe" arrivals · emergency alerts delivered · completed check-ins · live tracking sessions · escalation acknowledgments
Why this composite works
A user who shares one trip a day is the goal. The composite catches both daily habit (Safe Arrival) and safety-net usage (SOS) in one number.

Three supporting metrics.

Each supports the NSM and isolates a different failure mode.

01
Emergency Alert Delivery Success

% of SOS alerts successfully delivered to trusted contacts within 5 seconds.

formula =
(alerts delivered ÷ alerts triggered) × 100

Measures infrastructure reliability and notification speed. If alerts fail, every other metric drops.

Target · ≥ 99.5%
02
Check-In Completion Rate

% of scheduled or manual safety check-ins completed successfully by users.

formula =
(check-ins completed ÷ scheduled) × 100

Measures habit formation — the daily Safe Arrival use that builds the trust SOS depends on.

Target · > 90%
03
Trusted Contact Engagement

% of trusted contacts who acknowledge alerts, view tracking, or respond during escalations.

formula =
(engaged contacts ÷ alerted) × 100

Measures network effectiveness — LifeLine's value depends on contacts actually participating.

Target · > 80%
Note on MVP vs production

Instrumented in design. Wired in V2.

The MVP build does not emit telemetry events — there's no backend. But every event in this framework has a defined hook in the client code: on_sos_activated · on_arrival_confirmed · on_contact_alert_delivered. When we wire Supabase + Segment in V2, these fire automatically. The measurement plan precedes the measurement system.

Cross-reference
  • Metrics & Tracking Plan (doc 06) — full event dictionary, dashboard mockups, weekly review cadence.
  • PRD (doc 03) — non-functional requirements list alert latency SLAs and trust thresholds.
  • GTM Plan (doc 07) — pilot rollout includes baseline NSM measurement at week 1 vs week 8.
  • This document (doc 05) — confirms the build itself supports the measurement plan.

Known Limitations · Tech Debt

Honest list. V2 plan alongside.

Every limitation has a documented mitigation (today) and a planned fix (V2). No surprises.

Pri
Limitation
V2 fix
Owner
P0
No persistence — page refresh resets all state, contacts, trip history
Wire Supabase (same as SafeSignal) · user table · contacts table · trips table · alerts table
Engineering
P0
Alerts simulated — no actual SMS / push notifications fire to contacts
Integrate Termii or Africa's Talking for SMS · web push for in-app · email fallback
Engineering
P1
Map tiles need internet — embedded library is offline-safe but tiles come from OSM
Service worker tile caching · pre-cache user's home neighbourhood · IndexedDB tile store
Engineering
P1
Shake-to-SOS shows hint only — full SOS still requires 3-second hold
Intentional · prevents false alarms from pocket bumps · keep as design choice for V2
Design
P1
No event telemetry — metrics framework defined but no events fire
Wire Segment or PostHog · all event hooks already in client code · auto-fire on V2 backend
Engineering
P2
Test device coverage — iPhone 13 mini + Tecno Camon 19 only
BrowserStack mobile test matrix · cover Samsung A-series, Infinix, more iOS
QA
P2
English-only — Yoruba, Igbo, Hausa, Pidgin not supported
Externalise copy strings already done · add language picker · community-sourced translations
Product
P2
No background tracking — closing the app stops trip session
Service worker keeps session alive · resume on relaunch · push notification if backgrounded too long
Engineering
V2 Roadmap

What ships next.

Three priority bands. The MVP works on its own — V2 turns it from "demonstrable" to "deployable for pilot."

P0 · Must-have for pilot
  • Supabase backend (auth + persistence)
  • Real SMS via Termii / Africa's Talking
  • Web push notifications
  • Event telemetry wired (Segment/PostHog)
P1 · Strong-want
  • Service worker · offline tile cache
  • Background tracking · session persistence
  • Family-plan tier · shared dashboard
  • Onboarding A/B test framework
P2 · Nice-to-have
  • Yoruba · Igbo · Hausa · Pidgin localisation
  • Voice activation / panic word
  • Wearable companion (Apple Watch / Android Wear)
  • Integration · LASEMA / FRSC emergency dispatch
LifeLine · Build Documentation
Twelve screens · thirty tests · two iterations · one promise — Group 39, Phoenix Cohort, May 2026
